title: Inside the Decoder Block
concept: The decoder block is similar to the encoder but adds a third layer: cross-attention, which reads representations from the encoder.

exercises.prompt: What is the main job of the decoder in a transformer model?

options.text: To generate the output text or translation
To compress files into zip folders

exercises.explain: The decoder is responsible for generating the output, like translating or answering questions.

cards.title: The Writing Partner
cards.body: We know the encoder understands incoming text, but how does the decoder actually write the response while keeping the input in mind? It uses a special bridge called cross-attention! Let's peek inside the decoder block to see how it works.

cards.title: What is the Decoder Block?
cards.body: The decoder block is a special structure in AI models that generates new text. It is very similar to the encoder block, but it contains a third critical layer called cross-attention. This extra layer acts as a bridge, allowing the decoder to read and use the clues sent over by the encoder.

cards.title: The Translator's Notes
cards.body: Imagine you are translating a book from French to English. The encoder reads the French text and writes down summary notes. As the decoder, you write the English version. You use your own previously written English words (self-attention) but constantly glance back at the French notes (cross-attention) to make sure you stay on track!

cards.title: Decoder in Action
cards.body: When you ask an AI to translate 'Hello' to Spanish, the encoder processes 'Hello'. The decoder then starts writing. To generate the Spanish word 'Hola', its cross-attention layer looks back at the encoder's representation of 'Hello' to make sure the translation is accurate.

cards.title: Are They Identical?
cards.body: Some think the decoder is just a copy of the encoder. In reality, the encoder only looks at the input text. The decoder has the extra job of looking at both what it has already written AND the encoder's notes!

cards.title: Encoder vs. Decoder
cards.body: Let's compare the structure of the encoder block and the decoder block to see the extra layer.

left.text: Contains 2 main layers: Self-Attention and Feed-Forward. It only reads the input.

right.text: Contains 3 main layers: Self-Attention, Cross-Attention (to read the encoder), and Feed-Forward.

exercises.prompt: What are the three main layers inside a decoder block?

options.text: It only has a feed-forward network.
It has self-attention and nothing else.
It has self-attention, cross-attention, and a feed-forward network.

exercises.explain: The decoder block has three main layers: self-attention (to look at what it has already written), cross-attention (to read the encoder's notes), and a feed-forward network (to process the final details).

exercises.prompt: True or False: Both the encoder and decoder blocks contain a cross-attention layer.
exercises.explain: The encoder block only analyzes input and does not have a cross-attention layer. Only the decoder block has cross-attention to look back at the encoder.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing layer that connects the decoder to the encoder's output.
exercises.sentence: The decoder block uses a layer called [blank] to read representations from the encoder.
exercises.explain: The cross-attention layer is the special third layer that connects the decoder to the encoder's information.

exercises.prompt: Identify the correct description of a decoder block.

options.text: I am a decoder block! I only look at my own previous words and completely ignore whatever the encoder processed.
I am a decoder block! I use cross-attention to read the encoder's outputs while generating my own words.
I am a decoder block! I only have cross-attention and feed-forward layers, with no way to remember my own past words.

exercises.explain: AI B uses cross-attention to read the encoder's notes, which is the correct function of the decoder block. AI A incorrectly claims it ignores the encoder, and AI C incorrectly claims it has no self-attention.

exercises.prompt: You are building a custom language translator. Why does the decoder block have one more attention layer than the encoder block?

options.text: The encoder block uses cross-attention, but the decoder does not.
The decoder block uses cross-attention to connect with the encoder's clues, while the encoder does not.
Both blocks are completely identical and have the same three layers.
The decoder only has a feed-forward layer and nothing else.

exercises.explain: A decoder block needs self-attention to remember its own generated output, cross-attention to look at the encoder's clues, and a feed-forward network to process the final words. The encoder does not use cross-attention because it has no translation partner to listen to!
